title: STANDARD FOR FRESH AND QUICK FROZEN RAW SCALLOP PRODUCTS CODEX STAN 315-2014 Adopted in 2014. Revised in 2017. Amended in 2016.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR FRESH AND QUICK FROZEN RAW SCALLOP PRODUCTS CODEX STAN 315-2014 Adopted in 2014. Revised in 2017. Amended in 2016.
sections.summary: Document title and metadata only: Standard for Fresh and Quick Frozen Raw Scallop Products (Codex Stan 315‑2014) with adoption, revision and amendment years; contains no substantive provisions in this entry.

Standard applies to Pectinidae (scallop) products: (i) Fresh or Quick Frozen Scallop Meat (adductor muscle only), (ii) Fresh or Quick Frozen Roe‑on Scallop Meat (adductor with attached roe), and (iii) Quick Frozen versions of these with added water and/or water‑phosphate solutions; products may be for direct consumption or further processing. It excludes formed/mixed/bound scallop meat and whole scallops with shell/viscera (these are covered by CODEX STAN 292‑2008).

Section provides product definitions (Scallop Meat, Roe‑on Scallop Meat, and quick‑frozen variants with added water/phosphates), describes hygienic post‑harvest processing and freezing requirements (rinsing, draining, minimizing water uptake, storage at ≤4°C, rapid passage through maximum crystallization per CAC/RCP 8‑1976), allows controlled repacking/re‑freezing, and sets presentation/labeling rules including count‑per‑weight and labeling for packs with >5% broken pieces.

Defines three product types: Scallop Meat (adductor muscle only, no added water/phosphates/ingredients), Roe‑on Scallop Meat (adductor with attached roe, other viscera removed as practicable, no added ingredients), and quick‑frozen versions of these that may contain added water and/or phosphate solutions and optionally salt.
Fresh or quick‑frozen Scallop Meat is the adductor muscle completely removed from shell and freed of viscera and roe, presented whole and containing no added water, phosphates or other ingredients.

Fresh or quick‑frozen Roe‑on Scallop Meat is the adductor muscle with the roe left attached and other viscera removed to the extent practical, presented whole and containing no added water, phosphates or other ingredients.

sections.section: 2.1.3 Quick Frozen Scallop Meat or Quick Frozen Roe-on Scallop Meat Processed with Added Water and/or with Solution of Water and Phosphates
sections.summary: Quick frozen Scallop Meat or Roe‑on Scallop Meat processed with added water and/or water‑phosphate solutions consist of the defined adductor (and roe) products combined with a solution of water and/or phosphates and optionally salt.

Specifies processing requirements: after hygienic preparation products must be rinsed, drained and stored to minimize water uptake, fresh product kept at ≤4°C, and freezing must rapidly pass the maximum crystallization range per CAC/RCP 8‑1976; controlled repacking with reapplication of quick freezing is permitted and packaging should minimize dehydration/oxidation. For products with added water/phosphate the addition method (soaking, spraying) must be controlled, the amount measured for labeling, and the same freezing and repacking principles apply.

After hygienic preparation, Scallop Meat and Roe‑on Scallop Meat must be rinsed, drained and stored to minimize water absorption, kept at ≤4°C when fresh, frozen using equipment/procedures that pass the maximum crystallization range quickly per CAC/RCP 8‑1976, and may be repacked under controlled conditions with re‑freezing while minimizing dehydration and oxidation.

sections.section: 2.2.2 Quick Frozen Scallop Meat or Quick Frozen Roe-on Scallop Meat Processed with Added Water and/or Solution of Water and Phosphates
sections.summary: For quick‑frozen scallop products processed with added water/phosphate solutions, the same hygiene, rinsing, draining and ≤4°C fresh storage requirements apply, the added solution (e.g., soaked or sprayed) must be controlled and accurately measured for labeling, and freezing/repacking must meet rapid crystallization and quality‑preservation requirements per CAC/RCP 8‑1976.

Any presentation is permitted if the product meets the Standard and is adequately described on the label to avoid misleading consumers; packs may be by count per unit weight and if broken pieces exceed 5% of sample weight the product must be presented and labeled as 'pieces' or equivalent.

Products must be prepared from sound, wholesome scallops suitable for fresh or quick‑frozen sale; added water/phosphate/salt are permitted in quick‑frozen products only if water uptake is accurately measured and labeled and use is acceptable under the law/custom of the sale country, with potable water, food‑grade phosphates and salt complying with CODEX STAN 150‑1985. If glazed, glazing water must be potable or clean, and final product conformity is determined by examination methods in section 8 and lot compliance per section 10 against provisions in section 9.

sections.section: 3.1 Scallop Meat and Roe-on Scallop Meat
sections.summary: Scallop Meat and Roe‑on Scallop Meat must be prepared from sound, wholesome scallops of quality suitable for sale fresh for direct human consumption.

sections.section: 3.2 Quick Frozen Scallop meat, or Quick Frozen Roe-on Scallop Meat with Added Water and/or Solution of Water and Phosphates
sections.summary: Quick‑frozen Scallop Meat and Roe‑on Scallop Meat with added water/phosphate solution must be prepared from sound, wholesome scallops suitable for quick‑frozen sale; added water/phosphate/salt are permitted only if water uptake is accurately measured and labeled, water is potable, phosphates are food‑grade, and salt complies with CODEX STAN 150‑1985.
If products are glazed, the water used for glazing or for glazing solutions must be potable or clean as defined in the Code of Practice for Fish and Fishery Products (CAC/RCP 52‑2003).

Products shall be considered to meet this Standard when lots examined in accordance with section 10 comply with the provisions of section 9, and products shall be examined using the methods specified in section 8.

No food additives are permitted in Scallop Meat and Roe‑on Scallop Meat (sections 2.1.1 and 2.1.2). Acidity regulators, humectants, sequestrants and stabilizers may be used in quick‑frozen scallop products processed with phosphates if used in accordance with Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CODEX STAN 192‑1995) for food category 09.2.1 and its parent categories.

Products must comply with the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CODEX STAN 193‑1995) and CAC veterinary drug MRLs, and must not contain marine biotoxins above levels in section I‑5.2 of CODEX STAN 292‑2008 with sampling/analysis per that Standard. While scallop meat prepared per CAC/RCP 52‑2003 is generally not reasonably likely to present a marine biotoxin hazard (subject to species/data‑based hazard analysis), roe‑on scallop meat may present a possible hazard and requires preventive measures per CODEX STAN 292‑2008.

Preparation and handling should follow General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1‑1969) and relevant Codex texts (e.g., CAC/RCP 52‑2003, CAC/RCP 8‑1976, CAC/GL 79‑2012, CAC/GL 73‑2010), and products should comply with any microbiological criteria established per CAC/GL 21‑1997.

In addition to the General Standard for Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CODEX STAN 1‑1985), names must follow local law/custom and for added‑water products include 'added water'; products must be identified by common and/or scientific names as determined by the competent authority, presentation form must be described adjacent to the product name, and added water must be declared in the ingredient list with percentages of scallop meat and added water shown. Net contents of glazed products exclude glaze, labels should state storage instructions (≤4°C fresh, ≤‑18°C frozen), and non‑retail containers must carry required information on the container or accompanying documents with name/address/lot and storage instructions always on the container.

Product name must follow the law or custom of the country of sale and for 2.1.3 products must include 'added water'; the product must also be identified by common and/or scientific names as determined by the competent authority, the form of presentation per section 2.3 must be shown near the name to avoid misleading consumers, and added water must be declared in the ingredients with the percentages of scallop meat and added water clearly indicated.
For glazed products the declared net contents must exclude the weight of the glaze.
Labels should state storage instructions: fresh products must be stored at 4°C or below and frozen products processed per subsection 2.2 must be stored at −18°C or below.

Required information may be on the container or accompanying documents, but the name of the food, lot identification, producer/packer name and address, and storage instructions must appear on the container; the name/address may be replaced by an identification mark linked to accompanying documents, and the product must be identified by common and/or scientific name as determined by the competent authority (with the country of sale able to require scientific names).

Overview of sampling, examination and analysis requirements including use of attribute sampling (AQL 6.5%) for sensory/physical exams, s-method plans for net weight, AOAC methods for net/drained weight of frozen products, procedures for parasite, viscera/roe and added-water determinations, and references to CAC sampling and sensory guidelines.

Specifies sampling plans: attribute sampling per CAC/GL 50-2004 Section 4.2 Table 10 using AQL 6.5% for sensory/physical exams and sampling by variables with unknown standard deviation (s-method) per CAC/GL 50-2004 Section 4.3 Table 14 for net weight.

sections.section: 8.1.1 Sensory and Physical Examination and Determination of Added Water
sections.summary: Adopts the attribute sampling plan from CAC/GL 50-2004 Section 4.2 Table 10 using AQL 6.5% for sensory and physical examinations and for determining added water.
Requires use of sampling plans by variables with unknown standard deviation (s-method) per CAC/GL 50-2004 Section 4.3 Table 14 to determine net weight.

Samples for sensory and physical examination must be assessed by trained personnel following procedures in sections 8.3–8.7, the Annexes, and the Guidelines for the Sensory Evaluation of Fish and Shellfish in Laboratories (CAC/GL 31-1999).

Defines a scallop "piece" as scallop meat weighing less than 50% of the average weight of 10 randomly selected unbroken scallop meats in the package and provides a formula to calculate the percentage of pieces in a sample unit.

If count is declared, determine it by counting whole scallop meats (excluding pieces), then divide by the adjusted de-glazed weight (actual de-glazed weight minus weight of de-glazed pieces) to obtain count per unit weight.

Net weight must be determined by AOAC Official Method 963.18; for block-frozen products use AOAC 967.13 or 970.60 for drained weight, and block-frozen scallops must be thawed inside waterproof bags to avoid contact with and absorption of thaw water.
Examination for parasites is by normal visual inspection to detect readily visible parasites in the sample unit.

sections.section: 8.7 Determination of the presence of viscera and roe
sections.summary: Scallop products must be examined for remaining viscera attached to the adductor muscle or loose in the package and for remaining roe in scallop meat products.

To verify compliance with subsections 3.1 and 3.2, a country may establish a scientifically supported criterion for the natural moisture level of each scallop species and may consult importing countries to implement species-specific criteria.
Heading stating that a sample unit is considered defective when it exhibits any of the properties defined in the following subsections.

Deep dehydration is defined as more than 10% of the scallop meat weight or surface area showing excessive moisture loss (white/yellow abnormality) penetrating below the surface and not removable by scraping without unduly affecting appearance.

Foreign matter is any material not derived from scallops that does not pose a health threat but is readily recognizable without magnification or present at levels indicating non-compliance with good manufacturing and sanitation practices.
Scallop meat exhibiting persistent or distinct objectionable odours, flavours, textures or colours indicative of decomposition, rancidity, or other non-characteristic defects is considered defective.
Presence of parasites at an objectionable level renders the sample unit defective.
Objectionable matter—such as sand, shell, or similar particles—that is visible when thawed or detected by chewing during sensory examination at an objectionable level is considered a defect.
Indicates a nonconformity where the measured level of added water in the product exceeds the amount declared on the label (added water declaration).

Specifies lot acceptance criteria: the number of defectives (per section 9) must not exceed the sampling-plan acceptance number from section 8.1; where applicable, count/presentation defects must not exceed the acceptance number and the average count per unit weight must fall within the declared range; the average net weight must be at least the declared weight with no unreasonable shortage in any container; and essential composition, quality, additives, contaminants, hygiene, handling and labelling requirements of sections 3–7 must be met.

Prescribes sensory and physical examination procedures for frozen scallop sample units: determine net weight (per 8.5), assess surface dehydration and percentage affected, thaw per 8.5 and inspect each scallop for foreign/objectionable matter and presentation defects and weigh affected portions, check pieces and count declarations (per 8.3/8.4), assess odour and parasites, and cook a 100–200 g portion (with additional portions if needed) to evaluate odour, flavour, texture and presence of sand.
This is only an annex heading (Annex A) and contains no substantive regulatory requirements in the text provided.
